skill.name: domain-layer-expert
skill.description: Guides users in creating rich domain models with behavior, value objects, and domain logic. Activates when users define domain entities, business rules, or validation logic.
skill.summary: Helps design rich Rust domain models with value objects, entities, events, and business rules.
skill.author: EmilLindfors

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged Markdown backticks, front matter, and domain-model prose as command execution, weak cryptography, reconnaissance, and obfuscation. Manual review found only instructional Rust examples and no executable scripts, network calls, secret access, prompt injection, or malicious intent.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Code Fences Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The detected backtick locations are Markdown fences around Rust examples for value objects, entities, events, business rules, and anti-patterns. They do not execute commands and do not request command execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Each location is a Markdown code fence for a Rust snippet. No shell language, command invocation, or dynamic execution path is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern Not Present
low_findings.description: The reported high-severity weak cryptography locations are front matter and prose describing domain models. No hash algorithm, encryption API, password storage, or cryptographic operation appears at those lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain the words 'domain models' and related design guidance only. There is no evidence of MD5, SHA-1, DES, or any other weak cryptographic use.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Reconnaissance and Obfuscation Patterns Not Present
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance locations are Rust getters, ID generation, and a heading. The obfuscation finding points to Markdown front matter, not encoded code or prompt injection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is ordinary Markdown and Rust domain-model sample code. No system probing, environment discovery, JSFuck, encoded payload, or instruction override appears there.

content.value_statement: Rust domain code often becomes primitive-heavy and spreads business rules across services. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code suggest value objects, entity behavior, domain events, and validation boundaries.
actual_capabilities: Identifies primitive obsession in Rust entities and suggests typed domain concepts.
Shows value object patterns with constructor validation and ergonomic conversions.
Moves business validation into entities and domain types where appropriate.
Suggests domain events for state changes that other layers can consume.
Contrasts anemic models with behavior-rich entities using Rust examples.
limitations: Provides design guidance and examples, not a full architecture review.
Does not inspect repositories automatically beyond files the assistant reads.
Uses simple illustrative examples that may need adaptation for production code.
Does not replace product owner input for business rule accuracy.

use_cases.title: Strengthen a New Domain Entity
use_cases.description: Turn a draft Rust struct into an entity with identity, validation, and behavior.

use_cases.title: Refactor Primitive-Heavy Models
use_cases.description: Replace raw strings, integers, and status fields with meaningful value objects and enums.

use_cases.title: Document Domain Rules
use_cases.description: Express business rules as domain methods and events before connecting infrastructure.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Simple Entity
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of one Rust domain type.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Rust entity for primitive obsession and missing domain behavior. Suggest value objects and validation boundaries.

prompt_templates.title: Create Value Objects
prompt_templates.scenario: Convert raw fields into safer domain types.
prompt_templates.prompt: Given these fields and business rules, design Rust value objects with validation, constructors, and useful accessors.

prompt_templates.title: Move Rules Into the Domain
prompt_templates.scenario: Refactor service-layer validation into entity methods.
prompt_templates.prompt: Find business rules in this service logic and propose domain entity methods that own those rules.

prompt_templates.title: Model Events and Aggregates
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced design for state changes and integration boundaries.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Rust aggregate for this workflow, including value objects, invariant checks, domain events, and public methods.

output_examples.input: A User struct stores id, email, and status as strings.
output_examples.output: The skill would recommend UserId, Email, and UserStatus types, then add methods such as change_email and deactivate.

output_examples.input: Order validation lives in an application service.
output_examples.output: The skill would identify rules such as minimum total and editable status, then move them into Order methods.

output_examples.input: A workflow needs audit records when user email changes.
output_examples.output: The skill would suggest recording a domain event when the email changes and exposing events for the application layer.

best_practices: Share the business rules and invalid states before asking for model design.
Ask for small refactors around one aggregate or entity at a time.
Review generated examples against your error types, persistence model, and API boundaries.
anti_patterns: Do not use it to generate infrastructure code before the domain concepts are clear.
Do not accept sample validation rules without checking real business requirements.
Do not force every field into a value object when a primitive is clear and harmless.

faq.question: Does this skill write complete Rust applications?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on domain-layer design patterns and examples.

faq.question: Can it help with domain-driven design?
faq.answer: Yes. It suggests entities, value objects, invariants, and events for Rust domain models.

faq.question: Does it require a specific web framework?
faq.answer: No. The guidance is framework-independent and fits hexagonal architecture.

faq.question: Can Claude Code use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: Will it run commands or modify files by itself?
faq.answer: No. The skill only allows Read and Grep, and its content is instructional.

faq.question: Is it safe for sensitive projects?
faq.answer: The reviewed skill contains no network calls or secret access. Still avoid sharing unnecessary sensitive business data.
